title: Platform Engineering & Internal Developer Platforms
description: Platform engineering is the discipline of building internal developer platforms (IDPs) that enable engineering teams to self-serve infrastructure, deployment pipelines, and operational capabilities. Popularized by Backstage (Spotify), this is a growing focus at FAANG and a common Staff+ system design topic. This file covers IDP architecture, building golden paths, Backstage deep dive, developer experience metrics, the platform-as-a-product mindset, and how SAP's ABAP platform (ADT, gCTS, Steampunk) compares to modern internal developer platforms.

sections.title: What is an Internal Developer Platform?

description.value:
An Internal Developer Platform (IDP) reduces the cognitive load on development teams by providing a consistent interface for infrastructure, deployment, and operations. The key insight: developer productivity is not about how fast a developer can write code — it's about how fast they can ship that code to production safely.

IDP core capabilities: (1) Service scaffolding — create a new microservice with CI/CD, monitoring, logging, and on-call rotation pre-configured. (2) Deployment pipeline — automated build, test, deploy with approval gates, canary analysis, and rollback. (3) Environment management — provision staging, canary, and production environments on demand. (4) Observability — pre-configured dashboards, alerts, and log aggregation per service. (5) Service catalog — discover all services, their owners, dependencies, and API contracts. (6) Secret management — store and rotate secrets with access control.

Platform vs. tooling: A platform abstracts complexity; tooling exposes it. A CI/CD pipeline is tooling. A self-service 'deploy this service' button is a platform. The platform team builds the golden path (the default way to deploy a service). Teams can deviate from the golden path, but they must maintain their own deviation. This 'paved road' approach balances autonomy (teams can choose) with consistency (the default is standardized).

SAP Bridge: SAP's ABAP platform (Steampunk, ADT, gCTS) is an internal developer platform for ABAP development. It provides: code scaffolding (ABAP ADT templates, RAP generation), CI/CD (gCTS pipelines), testing (ABAP Unit, ATC), and deployment (transport management). The difference: SAP's platform is technology-specific (ABAP-only). FAANG IDPs are polyglot (any language, any framework). SAP engineers who understand platform engineering can help build the bridge between SAP-specific tooling and the wider platform.

Backstage is the most influential internal developer platform framework. Originally built by Spotify, donated to CNCF. Backstage provides the developer portal layer that unifies all infrastructure tools, services, and documentation into a consistent UI.

Backstage architecture: (1) App — a React-based frontend with plugin architecture. Teams extend Backstage with plugins (CI/CD, monitoring, cost tracking, API docs). (2) Backend — a Node.js service that aggregates data from infrastructure tools (Kubernetes, PagerDuty, Datadog, Snyk). It normalizes data into Backstage's entity model (the Backstage Catalog). (3) Catalog — the core data model. Resources (services, websites, libraries) are modeled as entities with metadata, relationships, and lifecycle information. Entities are ingested from source of truth (yaml files in repo, API calls, custom processors). (4) Software Templates — Backstage's 'scaffolder' module. Teams define templates that create new services with pre-configured infrastructure, CI/CD, and monitoring. A developer clicks 'Create Service' in Backstage, fills in the service name, and Backstage creates the repo, CI/CD pipeline, K8s manifests, and monitoring dashboard.

Key Backstage concepts for Staff+ discussions: (1) Entity lifecycle — Backstage tracks whether a service is production-ready, deprecated, or archived. This enables automated cleanup of deprecated resources. (2) Ownership — every entity has an owner team. Automated escalation: if a service has no owner for 30 days, it's flagged. (3) Scorecards — Backstage can compute a 'service maturity score' based on criteria (has on-call? has SLO? has docs?). Scorecards drive adoption of platform standards without manual enforcement.

The golden path is the 'paved road' — the default way to build, deploy, and operate a service at your company. It is opinionated, predesigned, and maintained by the platform team. Teams can deviate from the golden path, but they take on the maintenance burden of their deviation.

Golden path components: (1) Repository template — the initial repo structure with CI/CD config (GitHub Actions, Buildkite, Jenkins Pipeline), Dockerfile, K8s manifests, monitoring config, and documentation template. (2) CI/CD pipeline — build, unit test, security scan, deploy to staging, run integration tests, canary deploy to production (5% traffic), full deploy. (3) Observability stack — Datadog dashboard, PagerDuty integration, log aggregation (ELK/Loki), distributed tracing (Jaeger/Tempo). (4) On-call setup — PagerDuty schedule, runbook template, incident response checklist. (5) Documentation — service README template, architecture decision record template, API documentation (OpenAPI).

Platform team anti-patterns: (1) Platform team is a bottleneck — every change must go through the platform team. Solution: self-service with approval guardrails (no-platform-team-in-the-loop for standard changes). (2) Platform is too restrictive — golden path is enforced, not recommended. Solution: allow deviations with cost/effort acknowledgment. (3) Platform team builds, nobody uses — the platform is built without customer research. Solution: treat the platform as a product — interview developers, measure adoption, iterate based on feedback. (4) Platform is undocumented — developers can't discover capabilities. Solution: Backstage service catalog or developer documentation portal.

organizationalDimension.title: Building a Platform Team
organizationalDimension.migrationStrategy: The platform team starts with 3-5 senior engineers who have experience running services in production. Phase 1 (months 1-3): Build the golden path for one service type (stateless HTTP service). Dogfood it with the platform team's own services. Phase 2 (months 4-8): Onboard 3-5 early-adopter teams. Collect feedback, iterate. Phase 3 (months 9-18): General availability. All new services use the golden path. Legacy services have a migration window. Phase 4 (months 19+): Platform team expands to include dedicated SRE, documentation, and product management. Golden path supports multiple service types (batch jobs, stateful services, event processors).

stakeholders.concern: Platform team costs money and doesn't ship customer-facing features
stakeholders.address: The platform team reduces the per-service development cost by 40% and reduces production incidents by 60% (standardized observability). Show the data from early adopters.

stakeholders.concern: The platform is too restrictive — we can't use the tools we want
stakeholders.address: The golden path is the default, not the only path. You can deviate at any time — we'll help you understand the operational cost of your deviation. Most teams find the golden path is faster than building their own.

stakeholders.concern: Self-service will lead to unmanaged services that we'll have to support
stakeholders.address: Self-service with guardrails — the golden path includes mandatory on-call setup, monitoring, and documentation. Teams that deviate must meet the same standards. Backstage scorecards enforce compliance.

organizationalDimension.adoptionPhases: Phase 1: Build golden path + dogfood (3 months). Phase 2: Early adopters + iterate (6 months). Phase 3: GA + migration of legacy services (9 months). Phase 4: Platform maturity — multi-service-type support (ongoing).
organizationalDimension.metrics: Month 6: 5 services on golden path. Month 12: 30 services. Month 18: 80% of new services use golden path. Month 24: 90% of all services on golden path or approved deviation.
organizationalDimension.antiPatterns: Platform team builds in isolation (nobody uses it). Platform team becomes a bottleneck (approvals needed for everything). Platform is undocumented (developers can't discover capabilities). Golden path is enforced, not recommended (teams rebel).

description.value:
Developer experience (DX) is the platform's product metric. If the platform doesn't improve developer productivity by a measurable amount, it's not worth building. FAANG measures DX through several metrics:

DORA metrics (Google — DevOps Research and Assessment): (1) Deployment frequency — how often does a team deploy? Elite: on demand (multiple deploys/day). Low: between once per month and once every 6 months. (2) Lead time for changes — how long from code commit to code running in production? Elite: less than one hour. Low: between one month and six months. (3) Change failure rate — what percentage of changes result in degraded service? Elite: 0-15%. Low: 46-60%. (4) Time to restore service — how long to recover from an incident? Elite: less than one hour. Low: longer than one week.

SPACE framework (Microsoft — developer productivity): (1) Satisfaction and well-being — are developers happy with the platform? Measured via surveys. (2) Performance — how fast can developers complete tasks? Measured via DORA + task completion time. (3) Activity — how much are developers shipping? Measured via PR throughput, code reviews completed. (4) Communication and collaboration — how well do teams coordinate? Measured via cross-team PR volume, design review participation. (5) Efficiency and flow — how often are developers interrupted? Measured via context-switch frequency, 'flow time' (uninterrupted coding blocks).

Platform-specific metrics: (1) Time-to-production — how long from 'git init' to 'first deployment'? Target: <30 minutes. (2) Self-service success rate — what percentage of developers complete a task (deploy, provision environment) without platform team assistance? Target: >90%. (3) Platform NPS — would developers recommend the platform to their peers? Target: >50.

details: FAANG Interview Tip: Platform engineering is a rising signal in Staff+ interviews at FAANG. The company wants engineers who can build platforms that multiply the productivity of 100+ other engineers. When asked about platform design: (1) Use 'golden path' language. (2) Reference Backstage as the industry standard. (3) Discuss developer experience metrics (DORA, SPACE) — not just feature delivery. (4) Mention platform-as-a-product — the platform team has customers (developers), not users. The Staff+ engineer who can design a platform that developers want to use — not one they're forced to use — is highly valued.
